content:
{
  "improvements": [
    {
      "id": "IMP-001",
      "description": "Define explicit acceptance criteria and rollout plan for the soft-migration WARN-on-raw-model-id behavior, including WARN cadence (every call vs. once-per-process), log destination, and a measurable sunset trigger for `LOA_FORCE_LEGACY_MODELS`.",
      "rationale": "L7/NFR-BC-1/R6 establish soft migration but leave operational behavior ambiguous. Without a concrete sunset signal (date, telemetry threshold, or cycle), the kill switch becomes permanent — explicitly the failure mode flagged in R6. WARN spam can also drown audit logs.",
      "location": "§3 (L7), §5.4 NFR-BC-2, §7.1 R6, AC-3.1",
      "priority": "HIGH",
      "confidence": 0.85
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-002",
      "description": "Specify probe-gate semantics in detail: probe payload/prompt content, cost per probe, what 'ping' means per provider (auth check vs. tiny completion), cache key scope (per-process vs. cross-process), and behavior under partial probe failure mid-run.",
      "rationale": "AC-1.2 and NFR-Perf-1/2 reference a probe but don't define it. Probes that issue billable completions could materially affect cost; an underspecified probe also undermines M6 verification. Cross-process cache (e.g., file-backed) vs. in-memory has different correctness implications for parallel CI runs.",
      "location": "§4.1 AC-1.2, §5.1 NFR-Perf-1/2, §7.1 R3/R7",
      "priority": "HIGH",
      "confidence": 0.9
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-003",
      "description": "Add an explicit JSON Schema sketch (or link to one) for the typed-error taxonomy envelope, including field types, required vs. optional fields, and the `message_redacted` redaction contract examples.",
      "rationale": "AC-1.1 names seven error classes but provides no schema. Downstream consumers (BB TS, audit queries M1) will need a stable contract; without it, divergence between cheval Python and BB TS is likely to recur — exactly what cycle-102 aims to prevent.",
      "location": "§4.1 AC-1.1/AC-1.7, §5.2 NFR-Sec-1",
      "priority": "HIGH",
      "confidence": 0.85
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-004",
      "description": "Clarify M1 verification methodology: define the audit query precisely, the data source (envelope index? grep over JSONL?), how 'silent' is detected programmatically, and who runs the 30-day query at cycle ship.",
      "rationale": "M1 is the headline cycle-exit invariant but its verification is described in one line. 'No operator-visible WARN line' is hard to assert from audit data alone unless the WARN is itself audit-emitted. Without a runnable query, M1 cannot be objectively gated.",
      "location": "§2.1 M1, §5.3 NFR-Aud-1",
      "priority": "HIGH",
      "confidence": 0.85
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-005",
      "description": "Specify collision and precedence semantics for `model_aliases_extra` beyond 'register-only': what happens with capability-class membership conflicts, fallback-chain references to undefined ids, and ordering relative to defaults.",
      "rationale": "AC-2.3/L8 only address `id` collisions. An extension that adds a model to a capability class, or references a fallback id that doesn't exist, creates load-time ambiguity. Mirrors the `protected_classes_extra` precedent only superficially.",
      "location": "§4.2 AC-2.3, §3 L8",
      "priority": "MEDIUM",
      "confidence": 0.8
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-006",
      "description": "Define behavior when fallback chain contains a model from a different provider that itself fails the probe gate, and whether fallback walk respects capability-class boundaries (e.g., can a `top-reasoning-anthropic` chain end at an OpenAI model?).",
      "rationale": "AC-3.2 describes fallback walk but doesn't address cross-provider fallback semantics. Some adversarial gates (Flatline cross-model dissent) explicitly require provider diversity; silent same-provider fallback could undermine the dissent property even while satisfying 'graceful fallback'.",
      "location": "§4.3 AC-3.1/AC-3.2, §4.2 AC-2.4",
      "priority": "HIGH",
      "confidence": 0.85
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-007",
      "description": "Add a concrete budget and abort policy for smoke-fleet runs: max cost per weekly run, behavior when a provider rate-limits or returns 5xx during the sweep, and a kill-switch for the workflow.",
      "rationale": "A5 assumes rate-limit/billing tolerance but provides no enforcement. Smoke-fleet hitting every (provider, model) weekly without a cost ceiling is a real footgun; AC-5.3 doesn't bound this. R4 covers false alarms but not runaway costs.",
      "location": "§4.5 AC-5.3, §8 A5, §7.1 R-series",
      "priority": "MEDIUM",
      "confidence": 0.8
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-008",
      "description": "Define the migration path for in-flight runs and external callers when Sprint 4 deletes `model-adapter.sh.legacy`, including a deprecation window, search-and-replace audit across the repo, and grep-CI guard preventing re-introduction.",
      "rationale": "L12/AC-4.2 delete legacy in the closing PR, but external operator scripts may shell out to it. R2 mitigation cites the kill switch, but the kill switch (per A5) is a resolver shim, not a re-creation of the legacy script. Callers expecting the file path will hard-break.",
      "location": "§4.4 AC-4.2, §7.1 R2, §8 A5",
      "priority": "MEDIUM",
      "confidence": 0.8
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-009",
      "description": "Specify the contract test for AC-1.3 attacker schema and define what 'pinned' means: schema-version, breaking-change policy, and how schema drift between attacker and defender roles is detected.",
      "rationale": "AC-1.3 mentions a contract test but no schema reference. Red-team attacker output drift was a root cause shape (#780); without a versioned schema, this AC can pass while the underlying fragility persists.",
      "location": "§4.1 AC-1.3",
      "priority": "MEDIUM",
      "confidence": 0.75
    },
    {
      "id": "IMP-010",
      "description": "Add an explicit success criterion and falsification test for the Reframe Principle / capture-fixture-first pattern in AC-5.1 — e.g., a checklist item or CI-enforced PR template field for sprint-bug PRs.",
      "rationale": "R8 identifies Reframe violations as a real risk but the mitigation (codify in protocol doc) is passive. A protocol document alone won't change behavior; an enforced checkpoint (PR template, sprint-bug label gate) creates the forcing function the document describes.",
      "location": "§4.5 AC-5.1, §7.1 R8",
      "priority": "MEDIUM",
      "confidence": 0.75
    }
  ],
  "summary": "10 improvements identified, 5 HIGH priority. Focus areas: probe-gate underspecification, soft-migration sunset enforcement, fallback semantics across providers, M1 verification rigor, and typed-error schema formalization."
}
